title: SarcasmBench: Towards Evaluating Large Language Models on Sarcasm Understanding
venue: Preprint submitted to INFORMATION PROCESSING & MANAGEMENT
research_questions: Have LLMs really made significant progress in understanding sarcasm?
Whether LLMs can perform better on sarcasm detection with a limited number of examples in a few-shot setting.
Whether GPT-4 could perform better if more contextual examples are provided.
Whether chain-of-thought prompting is sufficient for sarcasm understanding.
What capabilities multi-modal LLMs show for understanding multi-modal sarcasm.

datasets_used.name: Ghosh
datasets_used.size: 51,189 tweets; 7,804 noisy tweets filtered; Train 32708; Valid 8687; Test 2000; Sarcastic 19546; Non-sarcastic 23839

methods_proposed.name: SarcasmBench
methods_proposed.summary: A benchmark/evaluation framework for assessing large language models on sarcasm understanding. It evaluates eleven LLMs and supervised baselines on six text sarcasm datasets under zero-shot IO, few-shot IO, and few-shot CoT prompting, and also includes a multi-modal sarcasm evaluation on MMSD and CMMA.

methods_proposed.name: Prompting-based sarcasm evaluation framework
methods_proposed.summary: The paper reformulates sarcasm detection as a conditional generative classification task in which an LLM maps a prompted input text to either ‘sarcastic’ or ‘non-sarcastic’. Few-shot demonstrations are selected using KNN search over the training set, and outputs are compared to ground-truth labels using standard classification metrics.

humor_theories.detail: The paper frames sarcasm as involving a contradiction between surface sentiment or literal meaning and true intention, and argues that sarcasm detection is a holistic, intuitive, non-rational cognitive process rather than a step-by-step reasoning task.

evaluation_methods.detail: Precision, recall, accuracy, and F1 are used as evaluation metrics; for each method, the paper reports the average result over five random seeds on the test sets.
Normalized confusion matrices are used for error analysis of GPT-4 Turbo across six datasets and three prompting approaches.

headline_results.finding: In the zero-shot IO analysis, supervised PLMs substantially outperformed traditional DLMs, with RoBERTa averaging 71.3 F1 and DC-Net-RoBERTa 71.5 F1, compared with Bi-LSTM at 66.7 and AT-LSTM at 65.3.
GPT-4 Turbo led the LLMs in the zero-shot IO setting, with F1 scores of 78.7 on IAC-V1, 76.6 on IAC-V2, 39.8 on iSarcasmEval, 33.3 on Riloff, 76.5 on SemEval Task 3, and 82.2 on Ghosh.
headline_results.dataset: IAC-V1; IAC-V2; iSarcasmEval; Riloff; SemEval Task 3; Ghosh

headline_results.finding: Across prompting methods, GPT-4 consistently and significantly outperformed the other LLMs, with the paper reporting an average improvement of 14.0%↑.

Few-shot IO prompting outperformed zero-shot IO and few-shot CoT, with the paper reporting an average improvement of 4.5%↑; GPT-4’s iSarcasmEval F1 rose from 39.8 in zero-shot to 52.3 in few-shot, a 31.4% improvement.
CoT prompting reduced performance for several LLMs: GPT-4 dropped from 68.4 average F1 with IO prompting to 64.7 with CoT, Mistral from 55.8 to 47.5, and Qwen 2 from 53.1 to 39.7.
headline_results.value: GPT-4: 68.4 to 64.7, 5.5% decrease; Mistral: 55.8 to 47.5, 14.8% reduction; Qwen 2: 53.1 to 39.7, 25.1% reduction

headline_results.finding: On multi-modal sarcasm detection, Retrieval-LLaVA 1.5 achieved the best MMSD results with Acc 90.0, P 89.3, R 89.6, and F1 89.4, while Qwen-VL-Plus had the best CMMA F1 among the reported multi-modal LLMs at 56.1.

key_findings: Current prompting-based LLMs did not fully outperform supervised PLM-based sarcasm detection baselines across the six text benchmarks.
GPT-4 Turbo was the strongest LLM overall and led the LLM group across multiple datasets and prompting methods.
Few-shot IO prompting generally improved LLM sarcasm detection, and the paper found that moderate numbers of demonstrations, especially 2-shot, were more effective than adding many examples.

Few-shot CoT prompting was less effective than straightforward IO prompting for sarcasm detection, which the authors attribute to sarcasm being holistic and not well matched to step-by-step logical reasoning.
Error analysis showed that imbalanced categories and sarcastic samples were key sources of misclassification, with GPT-4 Turbo often struggling to correctly identify sarcastic instances.
In multi-modal sarcasm detection, zero-shot multi-modal LLMs were weak relative to specialized baselines, but Retrieval-LLaVA 1.5 performed strongly on MMSD when using retrieval-based demonstrations.
limitations: The study only employed standard prompting methods and chain-of-thought techniques, without exploring more refined prompting methods such as tree-based or graphical approaches.
The study did not consider variation in model performance on sarcasm across different contexts.
The authors state that future work should refine context processing mechanisms to enhance models’ understanding of sarcastic language.

human_samples.population: No newly recruited human evaluation participants are reported for SarcasmBench experiments.
human_samples.observations: The study evaluates models against existing benchmark test sets and reports automatic classification metrics.
human_samples.detail: The paper reports no new human-subject evaluation, survey, preference study, or live audience.

human_samples.population: Online debate/discussion commenters represented in IAC-V1 and IAC-V2.
human_samples.recruitment: Legacy Internet Argument Corpus datasets; this paper does not describe new recruitment.
human_samples.observations: IAC-V1: Train 1214, Valid 304, Test 417, Sarcastic 973, Non-sarcastic 962. IAC-V2: Train 4031, Valid 1008, Test 1481, Sarcastic 3260, Non-sarcastic 3260.
human_samples.detail: The paper describes these as benchmark datasets from online debates and discussions with sarcastic and non-sarcastic comments, but does not report participant counts or labeler details.

human_samples.population: Twitter users whose tweets were included in the Ghosh dataset.
human_samples.recruitment: Legacy dataset automatically collected from Twitter using users’ self-declaration of sarcasm/irony via hashtags such as #irony and #sarcasm.
human_samples.observations: Original Ghosh dataset described as 51,189 tweets, with 24,453 sarcastic tweets and 26,736 non-sarcastic tweets; the authors report filtering out 7,804 noisy tweets. Table statistics after splitting: Train 32708, Valid 8687, Test 2000, Sarcastic 19546, Non-sarcastic 23839.
human_samples.detail: Tweet counts are item counts, not human participant counts.

human_samples.population: Text authors in iSarcasmEval who provided and labelled their own samples.
human_samples.recruitment: Legacy iSarcasmEval shared-task dataset; no recruitment details for this paper.
human_samples.detail: The paper states that each iSarcasmEval sample is provided and labelled by the authors of the texts themselves, and that English sarcastic texts include ironic-speech category labels. It does not report contributor counts.

human_samples.population: Human annotators for the Riloff tweet dataset.
human_samples.recruitment: Legacy Riloff dataset; tweets containing #sarcasm or #sarcastic and tweets without these tags were presented to annotators after removing such tags.
human_samples.observations: The source dataset is described as 1,600 hashtagged tweets and 1,600 without these tags; table statistics used here: Train 276, Valid 100, Test 113, Sarcastic 77, Non-sarcastic 412.
human_samples.detail: The paper says tweets were presented to a group of human annotators for final labelling, but gives no annotator count or demographics.

human_samples.recruitment: Legacy SemEval 2018 Task 3 dataset collected using irony-related hashtags and subsequently manually annotated.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report annotator counts, annotation protocol, or demographics.

human_samples.population: Social-network or conversation users represented in the multi-modal sarcasm datasets MMSD and CMMA.
human_samples.recruitment: Legacy public multi-modal sarcasm datasets; no new recruitment described.
human_samples.observations: The paper reports results on MMSD and CMMA but does not report dataset sizes in the provided text; it characterizes MMSD as English and CMMA as Chinese in the results discussion.
human_samples.detail: Participant or creator counts are not reported.

judges.name: Precision, recall, accuracy, and F1
judges.detail: Model predictions are compared with benchmark ground-truth labels using P, R, Acc, and F1. The paper states that each method is run with five random seeds and the average test-set result is reported.

judges.name: Normalized confusion matrices
judges.detail: Used for error analysis of GPT-4 Turbo/GPT-4 across six text datasets and three prompting approaches; cells represent the percentage of class i classified as class j.

judges.name: Text authors in iSarcasmEval
judges.detail: The paper states that iSarcasmEval samples were provided and labelled by the authors of the texts themselves.

judges.name: Riloff human annotators
judges.detail: The paper states that Riloff tweets were presented to a group of human annotators for final labelling after sarcasm hashtags were removed.

judges.name: SemEval 2018 Task 3 manual annotators
judges.detail: The paper states that the dataset was collected using irony-related hashtags and subsequently manually annotated.

judges.name: Twitter users’ self-declaration hashtags
judges.detail: For Ghosh, sarcastic tweets are described as automatically collected from Twitter using users’ self-declaration of sarcasm/irony with hashtags such as #irony and #sarcasm.
The authors state that they conducted a thorough double-check of the Ghosh dataset and filtered out 7,804 noisy tweets.

generation_selection.scope: Supervised DLM/PLM baselines and random baseline
generation_selection.detail: These systems are evaluated as classifiers, not as free-form generation systems with candidate selection.

generation_selection.scope: Text LLM zero-shot IO sarcasm classification
generation_selection.n: One output label per prompted test input is implied; no multiple generated candidates are described.
generation_selection.detail: Prompts instruct the LLM to return only ‘sarcastic’ or ‘non-sarcastic’. No best-of-N generation or output reranking is reported.

generation_selection.scope: Text LLM few-shot IO sarcasm classification
generation_selection.n: One output label per prompted test input is implied; k=2 demonstrations are used in the main few-shot experiments.
generation_selection.detail: The two demonstrations include one sarcastic and one non-sarcastic example sampled from the training set via KNN search. Demonstration retrieval is not output-candidate selection.

generation_selection.scope: Text LLM few-shot CoT sarcasm classification
generation_selection.n: One output per prompted test input is implied; no output-candidate set is described.
generation_selection.detail: The paper evaluates few-shot CoT prompting but does not report any best-of-N selection.

generation_selection.scope: Impact-of-demonstrations experiment for GPT-4 and Mistral
generation_selection.n: One output per prompted test input is implied; k={0,2,4,6,8} demonstrations are varied.
generation_selection.detail: KNN is used to choose demonstrations similar to the test sample; this is context selection, not generation selection.

generation_selection.scope: Multi-modal LLM zero-shot IO for GPT-4V, Qwen-VL-Plus, and Wenxin 4
generation_selection.n: One output label per image-text input is implied; no multiple generated candidates are described.
generation_selection.detail: The prompt asks the model to analyze image and text and return only ‘sarcastic’ or ‘non-sarcastic’.

generation_selection.n: One output per image-text input is implied; no generated-candidate selection is described.
generation_selection.detail: Retrieval-LLaVA 1.5 uses a retrieval module to search for demonstrations, but the paper does not describe best-of-N output selection.

model_provenance.name: Random baseline
model_provenance.detail: Uniform random predictions sampled across the test set.

model_provenance.name: TextCNN
model_provenance.detail: Official implementation and hyperparameters used; exact code commit/checkpoint not reported.

model_provenance.name: LSTM
model_provenance.detail: Official implementation and hyperparameters used; exact implementation version not reported.

model_provenance.name: Bi-LSTM
model_provenance.detail: Official implementation and hyperparameters used; exact implementation version not reported.

model_provenance.name: AT-LSTM
model_provenance.detail: Official implementation and hyperparameters used; exact implementation version not reported.

model_provenance.name: BERT
model_provenance.detail: Used as a PLM baseline; exact pretrained checkpoint not reported.

model_provenance.name: RoBERTa
model_provenance.detail: Used as a PLM baseline; exact pretrained checkpoint not reported.

model_provenance.name: DeBERT
model_provenance.detail: Used as a PLM baseline; exact pretrained checkpoint not reported.

model_provenance.name: XLNet
model_provenance.detail: Listed as a PLM baseline and reported in Table 2 for some datasets; exact checkpoint not reported.

model_provenance.name: DC-Net-RoBERTa
model_provenance.detail: Dual-channel RoBERTa-based sarcasm baseline; exact code/checkpoint not reported.

model_provenance.name: Baichuan 2-7B
model_provenance.detail: The paper reports the model name/size but no checkpoint, snapshot, inference date, or access mapping; LLM methods are said to use official Python API libraries and Hugging Face Transformers.

model_provenance.name: ChatGLM 2-6B
model_provenance.detail: The paper reports the model name/size but no checkpoint, snapshot, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: ChatGLM 3-6B
model_provenance.detail: The paper reports the model name/size but no checkpoint, snapshot, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: LLaMA 2-7B
model_provenance.detail: The paper reports the model name/size but no exact Hugging Face checkpoint, chat/instruct variant, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: LLaMA 3-8B
model_provenance.detail: The paper reports the model name/size but no exact checkpoint, chat/instruct variant, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: Mistral-7B
model_provenance.detail: The paper reports the model name/size but no exact checkpoint, instruct variant, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: Qwen 1.5-7B
model_provenance.detail: The paper reports the model name/size but no exact checkpoint, chat/instruct variant, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: Qwen 2-7B
model_provenance.detail: The paper reports the model name/size but no exact checkpoint, chat/instruct variant, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: ChatGPT
model_provenance.detail: The paper names ChatGPT and references chat.openai.com, but does not report whether this is GPT-3.5, a dated ChatGPT snapshot, or any inference date.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4 Turbo
model_provenance.detail: The paper reports GPT-4 Turbo but no API model snapshot such as a dated gpt-4-turbo version and no inference date.

model_provenance.name: Claude-3-haiku
model_provenance.detail: The paper reports Claude-3-haiku but no dated Anthropic snapshot or inference date.

model_provenance.name: Retrieval-LLaVA 1.5
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a multi-modal LLM with a retrieval module for demonstrations; exact checkpoint and execution access not reported.

model_provenance.name: Wenxin 4
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a multi-modal LLM; the paper provides a Wenxin/Yiyan URL but no snapshot or inference date.

model_provenance.name: Qwen-VL-Plus
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a multi-modal LLM; no exact API/checkpoint snapshot or inference date reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4V
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a multi-modal LLM; the paper references the GPT-4V system card but does not report an API snapshot or inference date.

inference_budgets.scope: Traditional DLM and PLM supervised baselines
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: All experiments are stated to be conducted on an NVIDIA GeForce GTX 4090 GPU; learning rate 5e-5, batch size 32, epochs 30, dropout 0.5, weight initialization U(-0.1, 0.1).
inference_budgets.detail: The paper says official implementations and hyperparameters are used and that five random seeds are run with average test-set results reported. It does not provide wall-clock time or training cost.

inference_budgets.scope: Text LLM zero-shot IO prompting across six text datasets
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Not reported; no multiple candidates or best-of-N are described.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: LLM methods are implemented with respective official Python API library and Hugging Face Transformers; no cost is reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Prompts consist of task instruction plus test input. The paper does not report temperature, top-p, max tokens, decoding mode, retry policy, or how invalid outputs are handled.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Not reported; no multiple candidates or best-of-N are described.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: No API cost or runtime reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Main few-shot setup uses k=2 demonstrations, including one sarcastic and one non-sarcastic example, selected from the training set via KNN search. The encoding function f for KNN is not specified.

inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: No API cost or runtime reported.
inference_budgets.detail: For GPT-4 and Mistral, k-shot settings are k={0,2,4,6,8}; KNN selects examples similar to the test sample.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Not reported; no output-candidate selection is described.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: No API cost or runtime reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper evaluates few-shot CoT and provides an example with step-by-step rationale, but does not report decoding parameters or exact CoT demonstration count separately from the few-shot setup.

inference_budgets.scope: Multi-modal LLM prompting on MMSD and CMMA
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: No API, compute, or cost budget reported.
inference_budgets.detail: GPT-4V, Qwen-VL-Plus, and Wenxin 4 use zero-shot IO prompts. Retrieval-LLaVA 1.5 uses retrieved demonstrations. Dataset sizes/splits for MMSD and CMMA are not reported in this paper section.

human_baselines.scope: Text and multi-modal sarcasm detection performance
human_baselines.description: No human performance baseline is reported. Human involvement appears only through legacy dataset creation/annotation/self-labeling, not as an evaluated baseline under matched conditions.
human_baselines.detail: All headline comparisons are among random, DLM/PLM baselines, LLMs, and multi-modal LLMs/baselines using automatic metrics against dataset labels.

mitigations: The authors report double-checking the Ghosh dataset and filtering 7,804 noisy tweets, but this is a noise-filtering step, not a decontamination test.
Few-shot demonstrations are selected from training sets via KNN, but this does not mitigate prior exposure of public test sets.
contamination_risk.basis: The main evaluations use six widely used public sarcasm benchmarks, plus public MMSD and CMMA multi-modal benchmarks, many created years before evaluated 2023–2024 proprietary and open LLMs. The paper does not discuss training-data contamination, does not use newly held-out/private test items, and reports no decontamination audit or canary test. Because headline claims compare LLMs on public legacy test sets with unknown training exposure, contamination risk is conservatively assessed as high.

llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLMs are evaluated systems that generate sarcasm labels; they are not used to judge or score other systems. Results are determined by dataset ground-truth labels and automatic classification metrics.

reporting_gaps: No API snapshots, dated model versions, or inference dates are reported for ChatGPT, GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4V, Claude-3-haiku, Wenxin 4, Qwen-VL-Plus, or other LLMs.
Exact Hugging Face checkpoints, chat/instruct variants, and local-versus-API access mapping are not reported for open LLMs such as LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, Baichuan, and ChatGLM.
Token budgets, temperature/top-p, decoding method, max output length, retry policy, and handling of invalid/non-label outputs are not reported.
The KNN demonstration encoder/function f and retrieval implementation details are not specified.
The paper reports five random seeds but does not list the seed values or clearly separate how random seeds apply to API-based LLM prompting versus trained baselines.
Human annotator/contributor counts, demographics, geography, and annotation quality controls for the legacy datasets are mostly not reported in this paper.
For IAC-V1, IAC-V2, MMSD, and CMMA, this paper uses dataset labels but does not describe the labeler/source-labeling process in detail.
MMSD and CMMA dataset sizes, splits, and label distributions are not reported in the multi-modal evaluation section.
For multi-modal PLM comparison baselines in Table 6, the paper does not clearly state whether scores were newly run by the authors or copied from prior work.
No contamination or benchmark-memorization analysis is reported despite evaluating later LLMs on public legacy benchmarks.
No API/computation cost, runtime, or number of total model calls is reported.
